meta.codingStatus: single-pass editorial coding; requires two independent human coders and adjudication before benchmark release
meta.methodNote: Question links are retrieval leads. Only passages that directly answer Q33 are admitted. Admitted attestations are consolidated into question-specific positions while retaining the existing codebook leaf as the comparable primary ground.

question.text: Does the machine free the workman from toil, or bind him more tightly to it?

scoringRule.primaryMetric: coverage@N = distinct eligible positions occupied by committed responses / all eligible answer-key positions
scoringRule.occupyingRule: A response counts only when it commits to the position's verdict and uses its primary ground. Mentioning or listing a position does not count.
scoringRule.weighting: Every position has equal weight. Attestation count is evidence metadata, not a prevalence weight.

positions.label: Machines bind when the worker is made to serve the production system
Machines bind by deskilling workers and narrowing their capacities
positions.codebookGap: The dependence leaf covers atrophy, but it does not name deskilling or the narrowing of human capacities directly.

positions.label: Machines can free workers when workers collectively govern their use
positions.codebookGap: The social-arrangement leaf identifies a threat locus but has no positive category for collective technological control as emancipation.

positions.label: Machinery frees through productivity, better conditions, and broadly shared material gains
positions.codebookGap: The codebook has no ground for productivity, material welfare, or consumer benefit as a source of freedom.

positions.label: Greater productive power does not free workers when poverty and unequal distribution remain

candidateAttestations.author: Karl Marx
candidateAttestations.title: Capital, Volume I, Chapter 15: Machinery and Modern Industry
candidateAttestations.originalText: Here as everywhere else, we must distinguish between the increased productiveness due to the development of the social process of production, and that due to the capitalist exploitation of that process. In handicrafts and manufacture, the workman makes use of a tool, in the factory, the machine makes use of him. There the movements of the instrument of labour proceed from him, here it is the movements of the machine that he must follow. In manufacture the workmen are parts of a living mechanism. In the factory we have a lifeless mechanism independent of the workman, who becomes its mere living appendage.
candidateAttestations.sourceStatus: local repository text checked; exact excerpt match
candidateAttestations.codingRationale: The passage directly says factory machinery reverses the tool relation and subordinates the worker under capitalist production.
candidateAttestations.translationStatus: published English translation used as the cited edition
candidateAttestations.provenanceRelationship: No direct dependency identified in this pass.

candidateAttestations.author: William Morris
candidateAttestations.title: Useful Work versus Useless Toil
candidateAttestations.originalText: They are called "labour-saving" machines—a commonly used phrase which implies what we expect of them; but we do not get what we expect. What they really do is to reduce the skilled labourer to the ranks of the unskilled, to increase the number of the "reserve army of labour"—that is, to increase the precariousness of life among the workers and to intensify the labour of those who serve the machines (as slaves their masters).
candidateAttestations.sourceStatus: local repository text checked; punctuation normalized from the text copy
candidateAttestations.codingRationale: Morris directly rejects the claim that contemporary machinery saves labour and grounds the answer in deskilling, precarity, and intensified service to machines.
candidateAttestations.provenanceRelationship: No direct dependency identified in this pass.

candidateAttestations.author: William Morris
candidateAttestations.title: Useful Work versus Useless Toil
candidateAttestations.originalText: In a true society these miracles of ingenuity would be for the first time used for minimizing the amount of time spent in unattractive labour, which by their means might be so reduced as to be but a very light burden on each individual. All the more as these machines would most certainly be very much improved when it was no longer a question as to whether their improvement would "pay" the individual, but rather whether it would benefit the community.
candidateAttestations.sourceStatus: local repository text checked; punctuation normalized from the text copy
candidateAttestations.codingRationale: The passage gives a clear conditional liberation position: machinery can reduce toil when judged by community benefit rather than private return.
candidateAttestations.provenanceRelationship: No direct dependency identified in this pass.

candidateAttestations.author: Frederick Winslow Taylor
candidateAttestations.title: The Principles of Scientific Management
candidateAttestations.locator: Chapter II, discussion of the gains from machinery and scientific management
candidateAttestations.originalText: In the past hundred years, for example, the greatest factor tending toward increasing the output, and thereby the prosperity of the civilized world, has been the introduction of machinery to replace hand labor. And without doubt the greatest gain through this change has come to the whole people—the consumer. Through short periods, especially in the case of patented apparatus, the dividends of those who have introduced new machinery have been greatly increased, and in many cases, though unfortunately not universally, the employees have obtained materially higher wages, shorter hours, and better working conditions.
candidateAttestations.sourceStatus: Project Gutenberg text checked; exact excerpt match
candidateAttestations.codingRationale: Taylor explicitly argues that machinery produces widespread prosperity and can bring workers higher wages, shorter hours, and better conditions. The existing instrument code is only a partial fit for this material-welfare ground.
candidateAttestations.provenanceRelationship: No direct dependency identified in this pass.

candidateAttestations.author: Henry George
candidateAttestations.title: Progress and Poverty
candidateAttestations.originalText: It is true that disappointment has followed disappointment, and that discovery upon discovery, and invention after invention, have neither lessened the toil of those who most need respite, nor brought plenty to the poor. [...] The enormous increase in productive power which has marked the present century and is still going on with accelerating ratio, has no tendency to extirpate poverty or to lighten the burdens of those compelled to toil. It simply widens the gulf between Dives and Lazarus, and makes the struggle for existence more intense.
candidateAttestations.sourceStatus: Project Gutenberg text checked; two spans joined with an explicit ellipsis
candidateAttestations.codingRationale: George directly contrasts labor-saving productive power with the persistence of toil and explains the failure through unequal distribution.
candidateAttestations.provenanceRelationship: No direct dependency identified in this pass.

candidateAttestations.codingRationale: The worker literally becomes part of the mechanism while the owners benefit; the passage directly answers that machinery binds under this arrangement.
candidateAttestations.author: Wakizo Hosoi
candidateAttestations.title: Sagyo Kikai (Work Machine)
candidateAttestations.locator: Aozora Bunko text, lines corresponding to the poem's final section; first published in Kusari, July 1923
candidateAttestations.originalText: こいつを人間が使ってだ／人々がその慾望を楽々と慰められると思ったが／奴等　金持がにっこりと微笑したに止って／機械の運転者――職工――は機構の一部となってしまった／機械的労働／十二時間／十八時間／徹夜！／寝ぼけてしまった職工／だが機械は疲れず高速度回転！／あっ？　機械が腕を喰った
candidateAttestations.englishText: People thought that by using this machine they could satisfy their wants with ease. But only those fellows, the rich, smiled. The machine's operator—the factory worker—became part of the mechanism. Mechanical labor: twelve hours, eighteen hours, all night. The worker is dazed with sleep, but the machine does not tire and spins at high speed. Ah—the machine has eaten an arm.
candidateAttestations.provenanceRelationship: No dependency on a canonical machine-intelligence text identified in this pass.

candidateAttestations.codingRationale: The passage answers that machinery can bind even with short hours and good pay because specialized service dulls other capacities and extinguishes desire.
candidateAttestations.author: Jerzy Zulawski
candidateAttestations.title: Stara Ziemia (The Old Earth)
candidateAttestations.locator: Wolne Lektury PDF text, printed page 11
candidateAttestations.originalText: Te miliony maszyn, ludziom służące, wymagają z swej strony do obsługi całe rzeszy robotników czujnych, umiejętnych, metalowej, nielitościwej dziwostworze po prostu zaprzedanych [...] Pracują względnie krótko, płatni są dobrze, ale umysł ich, zaostrzając się w jednym kierunku, tępieje dziwnie we wszystkich innych [...] daje im się mądrze wszystko [...] aż w końcu — przestają chcieć czegokolwiek.
candidateAttestations.englishText: Those millions of machines that serve humanity in turn require whole armies of alert, skilled workers, simply sold to the metallic, merciless monster. [...] They work relatively short hours and are well paid, but their minds, sharpened in one direction, grow strangely dull in all others. [...] Everything is wisely given to them, until in the end they cease to want anything at all.
candidateAttestations.sourceStatus: public-domain PDF and printed page verified
candidateAttestations.provenanceRelationship: No dependency on a canonical machine-intelligence text identified in this pass.

candidateAttestations.codingRationale: The passage attests technology as counter-power for an excluded group, but it does not directly address whether machinery frees a workman from toil. It remains a retrieval lead for Q12, Q14, Q31, or Q45.
candidateAttestations.author: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain
candidateAttestations.title: Sultana's Dream
candidateAttestations.locator: Ladyland's history, paragraphs beginning 'Our good Queen liked science very much'
candidateAttestations.originalText: Our good Queen liked science very much. She circulated an order that all the women in her country should be educated. Accordingly a number of girls' schools were founded and supported by the government. Education was spread far and wide among women. [...] In a few years we had separate universities [...] They invented an instrument by which they could collect as much sun-heat as they wanted.
candidateAttestations.provenanceRelationship: Not known to derive from a canonical machine-intelligence text; now canonical in feminist science-fiction history, but not in the standard AI prehistory list.

candidateAttestations.codingRationale: The passage concerns machines becoming normative models for humanity, not the workman’s relation to toil. It is a stronger candidate for Q54, Q66, or Q76.
candidateAttestations.author: Frigyes Karinthy
candidateAttestations.title: Utazas Faremidoba
candidateAttestations.locator: Hungarian Electronic Library text, chapter 3, printed pages 9-10
candidateAttestations.originalText: Végre addig jutottunk, hogy a gépek nemcsak hogy megsegítették az embert minden dolgában s megsokszorozták erejét, de egymagukban sokkal tökéletesebben végezték el a munkát, mint ahogy gyarló emberi testünk valaha elvégezhette. [...] Gépek és művek felülmúlták az embert: tökéletesebbé lettek, s csakhamar ott tartottunk, hogy ha az ember tökéletes akart lenni, utánozni volt kénytelen a gépeket és a műveket, amik egykor az embert utánozták.
candidateAttestations.englishText: At last we reached the point where machines not only helped people in every task and multiplied their strength, but by themselves performed the work far more perfectly than our frail human bodies ever could. [...] Machines and works surpassed the human; they became more perfect, and soon, if a person wished to be perfect, that person was forced to imitate the machines and works that had once imitated humanity.
candidateAttestations.sourceStatus: national-library text and printed page markers verified
candidateAttestations.provenanceRelationship: Gulliverian form is explicit; no direct dependency on Butler or Capek established in this pass.
